title: Writing with shell gold
subtitle: Mixing and applying shell gold to vellum and paper
excerpt: Shell gold is powdered gold bound with gum arabic and pressed into a flat cake. Unlike gilded leaf, shell gold is applied like a thick paint: loaded onto a pen or fine brush and written or painted directly onto the surface. The result is a warm, slightly matte gold line suited to calligraphy and fine ornament.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), on the use of shell gold in manuscripts: preparation, consistency for pen writing, and application to vellum. Internet Archive scan available.

glossaryTerms.definition: A calligraphy medium made from powdered gold (or gold alloy) bound with gum arabic, traditionally stored and mixed in a mussel shell and applied with a pen or brush.
A tree-resin binder used in watercolour and traditional calligraphy media. Added in drops to shell gold to adjust flow and adhesion.
Rubbing a dried gold surface with a smooth stone or burnishing tool to compact and brighten the metal particles, producing a more reflective finish.

content.text: Shell gold is named for the mussel shells in which medieval scribes stored and mixed their powdered gold. The medium is alive and responsive in the pen: diluted to the right consistency it flows smoothly, leaves a clean edge, and dries to a warm gold that can be lightly burnished. It is distinct from raised gilding (gold leaf on gesso): shell gold is flat, slightly granular, and immediate. A letter written with shell gold sits on the page rather than above it.

items.name: Shell gold cake
items.notes: Available from specialist calligraphy suppliers. A genuine gold cake (24-carat or 23.5-carat) gives the warmest colour; the imitation versions use gold-coloured mica and read cooler.

items.name: Gum arabic (liquid)
items.notes: For mixing with the gold and adjusting consistency.

items.name: Distilled water
items.notes: For thinning. Tap water introduces minerals that can affect the gold's adhesion.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3
items.notes: Clean the nib with warm water before loading shell gold.

items.name: Hot-press watercolour paper
items.notes: The smooth surface allows the shell gold to flow without catching on paper fibres.

items.name: Agate burnisher or back of a spoon
items.notes: Optional, for a light burnish once the gold is fully dry.

content.text: Add two or three drops of distilled water to the
cake and stir with the end of a clean brush until the gold dissolves into a smooth liquid. Add one drop of

to bind the particles to the paper. Test the consistency on a scrap of the same paper: the gold should flow freely from the pen without blobbing, dry in under two minutes, and leave a clean edge. If the gold drags in the pen, add one more drop of water. If it spreads beyond the intended stroke edge, add one more drop of gum arabic.

Load the nib by dipping it into the shell gold and touching the excess off on the edge of the container. Write at a normal calligraphy pace, no slower than usual. Shell gold dries faster than ink; if you pause mid-letter the flow stops and restarts as a visible join. Write complete letter strokes without stopping.
Allow the gold to dry for at least 30 minutes. Hold a piece of glassine or tracing paper over the gold surface and rub gently with the back of a smooth spoon or an agate
tool. The burnish compacts the gold particles and increases reflectivity. A light burnish is usually sufficient; heavy pressure on flat shell gold can smear the stroke edges.

items.symptom: The shell gold is granular and not smooth when it dries
items.cause: Insufficient stirring when mixing, or too little water.
items.fix: Stir for longer on the next mix. The gold particles must be fully dispersed in the liquid before loading the pen.

items.symptom: The gold peels off the paper after a few days
items.fix: Add one more drop of gum arabic to the mix. Do not use plain water as the sole medium; the gum arabic is the adhesive that bonds the metal to the surface.

items.symptom: The gold looks patchy and dull even after burnishing
items.cause: The paper surface is too absorbent.
items.fix: Size the paper with a light coat of diluted gum arabic (one part gum to ten parts water) before writing. Allow to dry fully before applying the shell gold.
